text: 1. The 0.5C and 0.8C steels exhibited a duplex ferritic–austenitic structure, while ordered phases (B2 and DO3) and κ carbides formed in ferrite and austenite of 0.8C steel, respectively. The 1.2C steel was fully austenitic with some κ carbides. ![](images/6d835e6ca8efca62a7a5fdbd7be808cd4b66bb653842a22d0cfb2d6a9cc14876.jpg) <details> <summary>natural_image</summary> Microscopic view of a material surface showing layered striations and cracks, scale bar indicates 0.5μm (no text or symbols present) </details> ![](images/e920eb4832669a312a7a26bb3601385e276e6f4f9c77c63c33d9438790b93e07.jpg) <details> <summary>natural_image</summary> Microscopic view of a material cross-section with visible grain boundaries and a 0.5μm scale bar (no text or symbols beyond label) </details> ![](images/fa14b187042e911ff164d31391a0e253024b2b4393c08be7d4016d4eb8a99b05.jpg) <details> <summary>natural_image</summary> Microscopic view of layered material structure with 0.2μm scale bar (no text or symbols beyond label) </details> ![](images/0bdc4fc8790b1b35e2990788cf3d6d0ad50d08afa69fa98481e98e06196c005a.jpg) <details> <summary>natural_image</summary> Microscopic view of a material surface with visible grain structure and a 0.5μm scale bar (no text or symbols beyond label) </details> Fig. 7. Microstructural evolution of 0.8C steel (TEM) (a)–(b) ε 0.01: (a) planar slip (γ), (b) dislocations (α), (c)–(d) ε 0.18: (c) microbands (γ), (d) densely populated dislocations (α). ![](images/737c078d951995388f9e4be00f1e352754e459fecd8b1bb98b68becaf04781d6.jpg) <details> <summary>natural_image</summary> Microscopic view of a material surface with visible striations and a 1μm scale bar (no text or symbols beyond label 'a' and scale) </details> ![](images/0777e42c2320dc88ba8ce6fe2429369ea6f6aabd25b2ee9cb28f3596ceb8b9bc.jpg) <details> <summary>natural_image</summary> Microscopic view of a material's grain structure with 1 μm scale bar (no text or symbols beyond label 'b') </details> ![](images/96047f089754cc0be9cfc6b499a668b326e73e2c225698c5c2f7fed8129bb342.jpg) <details> <summary>natural_image</summary> Microscopic view of a material surface with visible striations and a 0.5μm scale bar (no text or symbols beyond label) </details> ![](images/1b62cd8c7cafbfbfdd4124c8e9cc91fa95b8ac99215e9437cf2bcc406691ba4d.jpg) <details> <summary>natural_image</summary> Microscopic image showing layered material structure with 0.5μm scale bar (no text or symbols beyond label) </details> Fig. 8. Microstructural evolution of 1.2C steel (TEM) (a) planar slip (ε 0.02), (b) Taylor lattice (ε 0.07), (c) well developed microbands (ε 0.3), (d) interaction of the microbands (ε 0.4).

2. With increasing C content from 0.5% to 0.8%, the yield strength (YS) and UTS of 0.8C steel are much higher than those of 0.5C steel. Meanwhile, the ductility of 0.8C steel is also higher than that of 0.5C steel. The 0.8C steel exhibited a highest tensile strength of 979 MPa, and a moderate strain hardening capacity. The 1.2C steel revealed a superior combination of strength and ductility (ultimate tensile strength of 946.7 MPa and elongation to failure of 56%), with a much more pronounced strain hardening than the other two steels.

3. The C–J analysis can be utilized to evaluate the strain hardening behavior of Fe–18Mn–10Al–xC steels properly. It was revealed that 0.5C and 0.8C steels exhibited a two-stage strain hardening, while 1.2C steel manifested a three-stage strain hardening.

4. The hardening mechanisms at different deformation stages were further verified by microstructural changes during tensile testing. The influence of microstructure differences on strain hardening behaviors was well understood. # Acknowledgment This work is financially supported by the Natural and Scientific Foundation of China (Grant no. 51474062).
